# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1 (3 marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 model]**

**Q: Define the term "Zone of Inhibition" (ZOI) in the context of microbial bioassays. Mention its unit and the standard instrument used to measure it.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Zone of Inhibition (ZOI)** is the circular, clear, bacteria-free region that forms around an antibiotic disk, well, or producer colony on an agar plate pre-inoculated with an indicator organism. It represents the area in which the diffused antimicrobial concentration is high enough to prevent microbial growth, exceeding the **Minimum Inhibitory Concentration (MIC)** of the test organism. The ZOI is measured in **millimeters (mm)** using a **Vernier caliper** or a **zone measuring scale (HiAntibiotic Zone Scale)** held against a **non-reflective black background** with reflected light.

---

### **Question A2 (3 marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 model]**

**Q: What is the significance of the 0.5 McFarland standard in antimicrobial susceptibility testing? Mention the CFU/mL it corresponds to.**

**Model Answer:**

The **0.5 McFarland standard** is a barium sulfate turbidity standard used to standardize the inoculum density of the test organism before performing antimicrobial susceptibility testing (AST). It ensures reproducibility of zone sizes and avoids false interpretations due to over- or under-inoculation. The 0.5 McFarland standard corresponds to a cell density of approximately **1.5 × 10⁸ CFU/mL**. It is prepared by adding 0.5 mL of 1 % BaCl₂ to 99.5 mL of 1 % H₂SO₄.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Agar Well Diffusion Method*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks] Describe the agar well diffusion method for testing the capacity of a microorganism to produce a biologically active substance. Include the principle, materials, and procedure in your answer.**

**Model Answer:**

**Principle (2 marks):**
The agar well diffusion method relies on the radial diffusion of a cell-free supernatant (CFS) containing a bioactive substance from a well cut in agar, into a medium pre-inoculated with an indicator organism. A concentration gradient forms, and a zone of inhibition (ZOI) develops where the local concentration of the bioactive compound exceeds the MIC of the indicator.

**Materials (2 marks):**
- Producer culture (e.g., *Bacillus subtilis*)
- Indicator organism (e.g., *E. coli* / *S. aureus*)
- Mueller–Hinton Agar (MHA)
- Nutrient broth, 0.9 % saline
- Sterile Petri plates, cotton swabs, cork borer (6 mm), micropipettes
- Laminar Air Flow cabinet, Bunsen burner, incubator (37 °C), Vernier caliper

**Procedure (3 marks):**
1. Grow the producer in nutrient broth (24–48 h, 37 °C, 120 rpm).
2. Centrifuge the culture (10,000 rpm, 10 min) and filter the supernatant through a 0.22 µm membrane to obtain cell-free supernatant (CFS).
3. Prepare a 0.5 McFarland suspension of the indicator organism.
4. Lawn-streak the indicator on a Mueller–Hinton agar plate using a sterile cotton swab.
5. Cut 4–5 wells (6 mm diameter) in the agar using a sterile cork borer.
6. Load 50–100 µL of CFS into each well; one well receives sterile broth as negative control.
7. Pre-diffuse at 4 °C for 30–60 minutes.
8. Incubate at 37 °C for 18–24 hours.
9. Measure the diameter of the zone of inhibition (in mm) using a Vernier caliper.

**(b) [7 Marks] In an experiment, the following zone of inhibition diameters were recorded (in mm) for the CFS of an actinomycete isolate tested against three indicator organisms:**

| Indicator | Trial 1 | Trial 2 | Trial 3 |
|-----------|---------|---------|---------|
| *E. coli* | 15.0 | 15.4 | 15.2 |
| *S. aureus* | 22.0 | 21.6 | 22.4 |
| *P. aeruginosa* | 9.0 | 8.6 | 9.4 |

**Calculate the mean ZOI for each indicator and comment on the spectrum of activity of the isolate.**

**Model Answer:**

For *E. coli*:

$$\bar{D}_{E.coli} = \frac{15.0 + 15.4 + 15.2}{3} = \frac{45.6}{3} = 15.2 \text{ mm}$$

For *S. aureus*:

$$\bar{D}_{S.aureus} = \frac{22.0 + 21.6 + 22.4}{3} = \frac{66.0}{3} = 22.0 \text{ mm}$$

For *P. aeruginosa*:

$$\bar{D}_{P.aeruginosa} = \frac{9.0 + 8.6 + 9.4}{3} = \frac{27.0}{3} = 9.0 \text{ mm}$$

**Comments (4 marks):**
- The isolate shows the **highest activity against *S. aureus* (22.0 mm)** → strong Gram-positive activity.
- **Moderate activity against *E. coli* (15.2 mm)** → moderate Gram-negative activity.
- **Lowest activity against *P. aeruginosa* (9.0 mm)** → poor activity, as *P. aeruginosa* is intrinsically resistant to many antibiotics due to its outer membrane and efflux pumps.
- The spectrum of activity is therefore **broad-spectrum** but with bias toward **Gram-positive bacteria**. The isolate is a **promising candidate** for downstream purification and characterization of the active compound.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Kirby–Bauer Disk Diffusion Method*

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain the Kirby–Bauer disk diffusion method for testing antibiotic susceptibility. Discuss the role of Mueller–Hinton agar and the 0.5 McFarland standard.**

**Model Answer:**

**Principle (1.5 marks):**
The Kirby–Bauer method uses standardized antibiotic-impregnated paper disks (6 mm diameter) placed on Mueller–Hinton agar pre-inoculated with the test organism (adjusted to 0.5 McFarland). The antibiotic diffuses radially, creating a ZOI whose diameter is measured and compared with CLSI standard tables to classify the organism as **Susceptible (S), Intermediate (I), or Resistant (R)**.

**Role of Mueller–Hinton Agar (2 marks):**
- Low sulfonamide, trimethoprim, and tetracycline inhibitors → gives reproducible results.
- Supports growth of most non-fastidious pathogens.
- Standardized pH 7.2–7.4 avoids pH-induced variation in antibiotic activity.
- Recommended by CLSI as the standard medium for AST.

**Role of 0.5 McFarland Standard (2 marks):**
- Standardizes inoculum at **1.5 × 10⁸ CFU/mL**.
- Prevents false small zones (over-inoculation) and false large zones (under-inoculation).
- Ensures reproducibility across labs and operators.

**Procedure (1.5 marks):**
1. Prepare 0.5 McFarland suspension of test organism.
2. Lawn-streak on MHA plate.
3. Place antibiotic disks using sterile forceps/disk dispenser.
4. Incubate 35 ± 2 °C for 16–18 h.
5. Measure ZOI diameter (mm) using caliper against a black background.
6. Compare with CLSI table → interpret as S / I / R.

**(b) [7 Marks] The following data were obtained in a Kirby–Bauer test of a clinical isolate of *Staphylococcus aureus* (ATCC 25923 control). Interpret each result using the CLSI cut-offs given below and decide which antibiotic should be the drug of choice.**

| Antibiotic | Disk Content | ZOI Measured (mm) |
|------------|--------------|-------------------|
| Ampicillin | 10 µg | 10 |
| Chloramphenicol | 30 µg | 24 |
| Ciprofloxacin | 5 µg | 23 |
| Tetracycline | 30 µg | 17 |
| Streptomycin | 10 µg | 14 |

**CLSI Reference:**

| Antibiotic | S ≥ | I | R ≤ |
|------------|-----|---|-----|
| Ampicillin (10 µg) | 17 | 14–16 | 13 |
| Chloramphenicol (30 µg) | 18 | 13–17 | 12 |
| Ciprofloxacin (5 µg) | 21 | 16–20 | 15 |
| Tetracycline (30 µg) | 19 | 15–18 | 14 |
| Streptomycin (10 µg) | 15 | 12–14 | 11 |

**Model Answer:**

| Antibiotic | ZOI (mm) | Interpretation |
|------------|----------|----------------|
| Ampicillin | 10 | **10 ≤ 13 → Resistant (R)** |
| Chloramphenicol | 24 | **24 ≥ 18 → Susceptible (S)** |
| Ciprofloxacin | 23 | **23 ≥ 21 → Susceptible (S)** |
| Tetracycline | 17 | **15 ≤ 17 ≤ 18 → Intermediate (I)** |
| Streptomycin | 14 | **12 ≤ 14 ≤ 14 → Intermediate (I)** |

**Conclusion (3 marks):**
The isolate is **resistant to ampicillin** and shows **intermediate susceptibility to tetracycline and streptomycin**. It is **fully susceptible to chloramphenicol and ciprofloxacin**. Among the two, **ciprofloxacin (23 mm)** shows a larger ZOI than chloramphenicol (24 mm — nearly equal, with ciprofloxacin having a higher susceptibility margin), and being a **fluoroquinolone**, ciprofloxacin is generally preferred over chloramphenicol (which carries the risk of aplastic anemia). Therefore, **Ciprofloxacin is the drug of choice** for this isolate.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract the 6 mm disk diameter** — the ZOI is measured **across the entire clear zone, including the disk**.
> 2. **Writing "mm³" or "cm"** instead of **"mm"** for ZOI units — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 mark for wrong units.
> 3. **Confusing ZOI with MIC** — ZOI is a *qualitative susceptibility measure* (mm); MIC is a *quantitative concentration* (µg/mL). They are not the same.
> 4. **Using "Muller–Hinton"** instead of **"Mueller–Hinton"** — Mueller is the correct spelling.
> 5. **Not pre-diffusing the plate at 4 °C** — examiners specifically check this in 7-mark procedural questions.
> 6. **Reporting ZOI = 0** for resistant organisms — KTU often uses *intermediate* or *resistant* classification instead of zero; explicitly state "Resistant (R)" with reasoning.
> 7. **In cross-streak method** — students forget to mention the **second perpendicular streaking** of the indicator. Both streaks (producer first, then indicator) are essential to score full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Biologically active substances (BAS)** include antibiotics, enzymes, vitamins, toxins, and organic acids; antibiotics are the most commonly tested.
- **Three principal methods**: Cross-streak (primary screening), Kirby–Bauer disk diffusion (gold standard AST), Agar well diffusion (CFS quantification).
- **Mueller–Hinton Agar (MHA)** is the CLSI-recommended medium; pH 7.2–7.4, depth ~4 mm.
- **0.5 McFarland standard** = 1.5 × 10⁸ CFU/mL; critical for inoculum standardization.
- **Zone of Inhibition (ZOI)** is measured in **mm** using a **Vernier caliper** against a **black background with reflected light**.
- **Pre-diffusion at 4 °C for 30–60 min** is a critical step to allow antibiotic diffusion before microbial growth.
- **CLSI classifications**: Susceptible (S), Intermediate (I), Resistant (R) — based on ZOI thresholds.
- **MIC** is the lowest concentration with no visible growth; **MBC** is the lowest concentration with 99.9 % kill.
- **Quality control strains** (ATCC 25922, 25923, 27853) must be run periodically for AST validation.
- **Cross-streak method** is a qualitative, primary screening tool — ideal for *initial detection* of antagonistic microbes from environmental samples (e.g., soil actinomycetes).
- **Standard curve** of *D vs log C* enables quantification of unknown antibiotic concentrations by interpolation.
- **Activity Index** = (test zone radius) / (standard zone radius) — useful for comparing potency of a new isolate against a reference strain.
- **Industrial relevance**: drug discovery, antibiotic QC, clinical microbiology (AMR surveillance), food preservation (bacteriocins), agricultural biocontrol.
- **Always mention controls**: negative control (sterile broth / saline) and positive control (known antibiotic) in any bioassay write-up.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When the question asks "what is the inference?", always state:
> 1. Whether the test organism **produces** an antimicrobial (yes/no).
> 2. The **spectrum** (broad/narrow).
> 3. The **potency** (strong/moderate/weak, based on ZOI size).
> 4. The **clinical / industrial relevance** of the finding.
> This 4-point structure is what KTU examiners look for in 14-mark Part B answers.
